notes:
- Key concepts introduced (with brief explanation)
  - team.run: runs the team synchronously / non-streaming — the call blocks and no intermediate output is shown until the entire run finishes.
  - team.runstream (or run_stream): runs the team in streaming mode — yields agent outputs incrementally as each agent finishes, allowing you to see partial results without waiting for the whole run.
  - team.reset: clears the team's context/history (like starting a new chat); after reset the team has no prior context.
  - max_turns (max turns): configuration to limit how many turns/rounds the team executes; affects how many times agents alternate (e.g., plot→character→ending repeated).
  - Single-person team vs multi-agent team: a team can be composed of multiple distinct agents (roles) or a single agent repeated across turns; both are supported and have different trade-offs.
  - Agent roles and round-robin flow: example roles used — plot writer, character writer, ending writer — which run in sequence and can repeat for multiple rounds when max_turns > 1.
  - Source/content metadata in agent messages: agent outputs include fields like "source" and "content", whereas older examples may lack that structure.
  - TaskResult / task result: a message/type returned by runs that contains status or stop messages (e.g., "maximum number of turns X reached"); appears to live in autogen.chat.base (import needed).
  - Use-case pattern: writer → reviewer → summarizer → writer flow (reviewing cycle) — useful when you want iterative improvement via multiple agent roles.
  - Tools in agents: agents in the team can be equipped with tools (briefly mentioned as possible).
  - Timing observations: non-stream run example took ~19 seconds; another example returned after ~13.5 seconds — streaming reveals outputs sooner as agents finish.

- Any analogies or metaphors used
  - "Like a new chat in chat GPD" — resetting the team is compared to starting a new chat session in ChatGPT.
  - "If you remember I asked it to get a coffee no communication whatsoever went and get me the coffee." — example/analogy to illustrate a lack of intermediate feedback and potentially undesired autonomous actions.
  - Working in a team requires a "very strong understanding" between participants — compared to human-team coordination to emphasize the need for strong prompting and protocol.

- Any code, commands, or config shown
  - team.run task = task  (conceptual snippet showing synchronous run usage)
  - team.runstream (run_stream) usage pattern: "async for message in team.runstream" — iterate messages as they stream in.
  - team.reset — command to clear team context.
  - max_turns equals to 6 (or max_turns=6) — example config to allow more rounds.
  - max_turns = 2 — example config causing "maximum number of turns two reached".
  - Mentioned import: from autogen.chat.base import TaskResult (or similar) — note: transcript phrasing: "from autogen agent chat. Base import... import task result... it is in autogen chat base".
  - Example message flow labels in streaming output: "source user", "source plot writer", etc. — implying message objects contain source and content fields.
  - Code idea: create team_two to represent single-person team variant (team_two = team) and run it similarly.

- Any warnings, gotchas, or important caveats
  - team.run blocks and gives no intermediate output — you cannot stop it mid-run if something is going wrong (no in-flight feedback).
  - For long flows, blocking behavior becomes irritating (you may have to wait 1–2 minutes for output).
  - If prompting is weak, agents will not behave well — need strong, precise prompts especially in multi-agent reviewer flows.
  - Resetting the team deletes all context — behaves like starting a new chat; useful but you lose prior state.
  - max_turns enforcement: you may hit a "maximum number of turns X reached" stop message (appears in task_result).
  - Streaming requires handling/consuming TaskResult/message objects (import from autogen.chat.base).
  - Setup effort: multi-agent teams provide more diversity but require more effort to define and coordinate than single-agent runs.

- Any comparisons or lists (potential table candidates)
  - Single agent vs Multi-agent (high-level comparison mentioned)
    - Creativity: limited (single) vs diverse viewpoints (multi)
    - Ideas: narrower (single) vs more varied (multi)
    - Depth/flexibility: less (single) vs more (multi)
    - Setup effort: lower (single) vs higher (multi)
  - Execution modes
    - team.run: synchronous, no incremental output
    - team.runstream: streaming, incremental outputs as agents finish
  - Example team role sequence (repeated per round)
    - Plot writer → Character writer → Ending writer (then repeat)
  - Use-case roles
    - Writer, Reviewer, Summarizer — typical iterative workflow where reviewer and summarizer feed back to writer.

- Direct quotes worth preserving
  - "Write a short story. Uh, write a short story. A brave boy at this Pokémon. Keep it up to let me make it 50 words only because else it will be a lot long, right?"
  - "run stream will make a lot of sense. Pay attention. It is running but it is not showing you any output."
  - "team.run task is equal to task."
  - "I could not have stopped it in between if something wrong was happening"
  - "if you remember I asked it to get a coffee no communication whatsoever went and get me the coffee."
  - "plot writer has given us a very good plot in Luminina town. Young Kai and his fairy Charmander venture into this."
  - "Charmander and the Master Umbra face to face."
  - "As Kai and Charmander celebrated their victory over Master Kumra, the burned shadows begin to swirl and reform, revealing a hidden message in the groves, ashes etc."
  - "If I give max turns equals to six ... it will take a lot of time. They will be getting two two turns each."
  - "your prompting should also be very strong here. If you make an error or very weak prompting you will see that they are not behaving nicely."
  - "team.reset ... just like a new chat in chat GPD where no context is there"
  - "async for message in team.runstream"
  - "This task result is something which we will have to import ... from autogen.chat.base ... import task result."
  - "maximum number of turns two reached" (as example stop message seen in TaskResult)
  - "task_result type of a message we get this message dots stop diesel right" (verbatim phrasing preserved)
